data.projectDetailsBranch: Integrity Services Branch and Temporary Foreign Worker Branch
data.projectDetailsTitle: Temporary Foreign Worker Program Case Selection Algorithm
data.projectDetailsDescription: A set of algorithms and business rules to prioritize TFWP cases for inspection based on the existence and severity of established risk factors.
data.businessDrivers3:
The Temporary Foreign Worker Program (TFWP) administers a compliance Regime. The compliance regime inspects employers who use the Program to verify their compliance with the program conditions of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Regulations (IRPR). The Program needs a system that can help generate a random list of employers to be inspected. In the past, these lists were made using purely random sampling methods, and more recently, a predictive model which took into consideration simple risk factors in the generation of random samples was used.

The new approach leverages additional data sources that can be used to flag new risk factors in accordance with compliance with the IRPR in the Labour Market Impact Assessment (LMIA) and informed by observations noted by inspectors. The new approach is to account for employers who are at higher risk of potential non-compliance with program requirements and to improve the reach of inspections. In addition, this new system will also contribute to the quality of the compliance regime by enabling improved data-informed inspections.
data.businessDrivers4: The TFWP compliance regime, including inspections, helps to maintain program integrity and to help protect temporary foreign workers rights, by holding employers accountable for any violation of program requirements. Through the inspections process, employer’s adherence to program conditions is verified, and administrative monetary penalties and/or bans from the program are issued to non-compliant employers. There are approximately 50,000 employers who are approved to use the TFWP, and only a percentage are selected for inspections, in any given year. Implementing this system would allow the Program to prioritize and select employers with the risk factors linked to potential non-compliance.
data.businessDrivers6: The automation will make it possible to both use a significant amount of data and will include the ability to quickly respond to changes in the needs of the TFWP. It will allow ongoing support of the needs of the TFWP, whether it be changes to risk flags or data repositories, or changes to the quantity and distribution of cases.
data.businessDrivers12: Given the TFWP's compliance regime is continuously evolving, it would be a missed opportunity not to explore this type of technology. As a result of not deploying the system, there could be serious integrity issues in the TFWP that could go unaddressed, and operational resources could be directed to inspect fewer cases of potential program misuse.
data.decisionSector2: Employers who use the TFW Program can be randomly selected for an inspection; selected based on tips or allegations of non-compliance; or based on past non-compliance with program requirements. If an employer fails to meet program requirements and/or conditions, consequences can include administrative monetary penalties and a temporary or permanent ban from the Program.
data.decisionSector3: The process will select employers who use the TFWP for inspection by Service Canada. The number of selections by region and stream (of the TFWP) will be determined by the TFWP Branch. Cases will be referred to the case management system, where human staff will take full control of managing, distributing, and carrying out cases according to relevant procedures, regulations, and guidance.
data.impact31: The system will support the decision-making process by generating a list of employers to be considered for inspection. As previously stated, the system will make random selections from among potentially "high-risk" employers and will also make a percentage of purely random selections from the entire employer population. The system will make no recommendations regarding the case to the human staff; from the point of referral a human will be responsible for all decisions made in the case. Some details of the specific risk flag the employer triggered may be communicated to staff (via the case management system), and if necessary and authorized, data will be provided. At all times the investigators will follow the established inspection procedures, regulations, and guidance, in accordance with their training to conduct compliance inspections on employers.
data.impact21: The system will produce a list of business numbers (BNs) and associated LMIA data that will be referred to the case management system for the creation of inspection cases. The output will consist of data in the same format used by other cases which are manually loaded into the case management system.
data.impact8: Upon referral to the case management system, the distribution, assignment, and initiation of an inspection will be carried out by human staff. They will maintain the autonomy to remove or delete cases, and follow all applicable procedures, regulations, and guidance. The system will make no recommendations regarding decisions in the inspection.
data.impact10: The IRPR and LMIA agreement state that any employer can be inspected under the random selection trigger. All employers using the TFWP may be subject to random selection for an inspection.
data.impact25: All employers may be subject to inspection at any time, and inspections will follow the existing established guidelines and procedures.
data.impact12: By selecting employers with more potential risk of non-compliance with IRPR, the automation may have a positive impact on the health and safety and security of temporary foreign workers, who work for participating employers. It is hoped that increased focus of inspections on these high-risk employers will lead to greater program compliance.
data.impact14: The outcome of the system will have no effect on employers using the program. The outcome of the inspection may have a negative financial impact on employers who are misusing the Program, as they may be fined or banned from the program according to the IRPR.
data.impact16: The automation will not impact the number of inspections initiated by the program, so operational activities such as travel will not be affected.
data.aboutDataSource13: The data collected comes from various Federal Departments and Provincial governments. The TFWP is a jointly administered program between IRCC, CBSA, and ESDC, with inspections conducted by Service Canada. Data primarily comes from those sources. In addition, there are other departmental data (ESDC) in use under DESDA, and Information Sharing Agreements (ISAs) with various Provincial jurisdictions for the purposes of TFWP Integrity.
data.businessDrivers10: Purely random sampling was considered. Random sampling will still be used for a percentage of employers selected for inspection, but the automated process, which will provide random selections among employers deemed "high-risk" will be primarily used. Incorporating risk of program misuse into employer selection is necessary for TFWP compliance to mitigate these risks. One of the goals of the automated process is to use all available data at its disposal and use automation to help connect data to increase focus of inspections on employers at highest risk of potential program misuse, and help inspectors detect and address employers' non-compliance more effectively. The automated process will allow TFWP to identify cases that are both more timely and relevant compared to those obtained through purely random selection.
data.businessDrivers7: The project consists of two case selection activities – random selection among high-risk employers, and random selection from the entire population of employers to serve as a baseline. There is also a process for modifying the parameters and data used as needed (dependent on internal consultations, monitoring, and data quality evaluation). The system is limited to selecting employers and referring cases to the case management system. All activities after this selection will be carried out by human staff following the regular operational procedures as set out in the regulations and internal guidance. This includes human autonomy, leaving the decision making to the delegated decision maker.
data.impact20: Disclosure of this criteria is restricted due to the nature of the solution.
data.privacyImplementation6: A formal privacy risk assessment was conducted by ESDC’s Privacy Management Division in accordance with established privacy principles, TBS policies and directives, and the Privacy Act and Part 4 of the DESDA, to ensure that privacy controls and safeguards were well-established, and privacy risks subject to an actionable plan for mitigation.
